items.q: What did Bowlby mean by monotropy?
items.correct: One primary attachment figure (usually the mother) is qualitatively different and most important.
items.misconception: A child can only ever form one attachment.
items.why: Students take 'mono' literally as 'only one'. Bowlby recognised multiple attachments but argued the primary one was uniquely important for the internal working model.

items.correct: Every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection.
items.misconception: Just grabbing whoever is around (any haphazard method).
items.random_wrong: Picking participants who match the population profile.
items.why: Students treat 'random' as 'casual' or 'haphazard'. True randomness requires a defined sampling frame and a chance procedure (e.g., random number generator).

items.q: Asch's line study primarily demonstrates which type of conformity?
items.correct: Normative social influence (compliance to fit in).
items.misconception: Informational social influence (believing others know better).
items.why: Students assume participants genuinely believed the wrong line was correct. Asch's debriefs showed they knew it was wrong but conformed publicly to avoid rejection - this is normative, not informational.

items.q: Working Memory was proposed in 1974 by:
items.why: It's Baddeley & Hitch. “Atkinson & Shiffrin” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Miller (1956) said STM capacity is around:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 7 ± 2. “3 ± 1” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 1968. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
